own_review.issue: Only forbiddenApisMain is wired into test.dependsOn; forbiddenApisTest is omitted, asymmetric with the existing checkstyleMain+checkstyleTest pattern on the same line. (Note the plugin does auto-wire all forbiddenApis* tasks into `check`, so the gap only affects builds that invoke `test` directly.)

bundledSignatures = ['jdk-unsafe'] is unversioned. The Gradle plugin resolves the signature version from project.targetCompatibility, which Kafka never sets (it uses options.release = minJavaVersion / 11 instead), so it defaults to whatever JDK runs the build. The checked signature set therefore varies by build JDK, and on a JDK newer than the plugin supports the check silently skips (failOnUnsupportedJava defaults to false). Pin 'jdk-unsafe-11' (or set targetCompatibility) for deterministic behavior.

Plugin id added without `apply false`, unlike spotbugs/scoverage/shadow. Since the root project also applies 'java-library', the plugin is applied to the root project where the forbiddenApis extension is never configured (the configuration lives only in subprojects{}). Inconsistent with build conventions; should be `apply false` given it is explicitly applied in subprojects anyway.
Trailing newline at end of file removed ('\ No newline at end of file') — accidental, unrelated whitespace change; base file ends with a newline.

ignoreFailures = true makes the check purely advisory; intentional per PR description and necessary because nearly every module currently has violations (matches reviewer discussion), but needs a tracked follow-up to ever become enforcing.

points.point: :core silently excluded — 'forbiddenapis resolves sources from the Java source set, forbiddenApisMain will have ZERO sources for :core'
points.note: Fabricated plugin semantics (the known :core build-semantics fabrication class). The setup facts are real (core sets java srcDirs = [] at build.gradle:1336-1352, 19 Java files exist), but forbidden-apis is a bytecode checker: the Gradle plugin runs CheckForbiddenApis on sourceSet.output.classesDirs. Gradle's joint compilation puts both the Scala- and Java-derived classes of :core into main's output, so :core IS analyzed. No coverage gap.

points.point: ignoreFailures = true non-enforcing; add follow-up to enforce
points.note: Verified; aligns with the human reviewer's discussion of ignoreFailures.
Verified real asymmetry with the checkstyle pattern on the same line.

points.point: Plugin applied unconditionally to all subprojects incl. code-less :connect
points.note: Factually true (build.gradle:297 comment confirms 'connect is just a folder') but checkstyle/spotbugs are already applied the same way; v5 itself says not harmful.

points.point: Only jdk-unsafe signature; plan more later

missed: Unversioned 'jdk-unsafe' depends on targetCompatibility Kafka never sets — signature set varies by build JDK / can silently skip

points.point: forbiddenApisTest not included in dependency chain
ignoreFailures = true never blocks; consider property gate for enforcement
points.note: Substance verified and aligns with human discussion; 'never produce actionable output' is slightly overstated (violations are still logged) but the core claim is right.

points.point: Missing trailing newline at end of file
points.note: Verified: base file ends with newline; PR removes it. Real unintended change.

points.point: No suppression mechanism: plugin supports suppression files via signaturesFiles and inline @SuppressWarnings("forbiddenapis")
points.note: Fabricated tool mechanics: signaturesFiles ADDS forbidden signatures, it is not a suppression file; and @SuppressWarnings has SOURCE retention so a bytecode analyzer cannot see it (forbidden-apis uses class-retained annotations like @SuppressForbidden via suppressAnnotations).

missed: Unversioned 'jdk-unsafe' depends on targetCompatibility Kafka never sets — signature set varies by build JDK / can silently skip

qualitative: Tiny build-only PR (forbidden-apis plugin, jdk-unsafe, ignoreFailures=true); the human's sole point validates ignoreFailures as necessary. v5's headline finding is the recurring :core fabrication class: it correctly reads the srcDirs=[] joint-compilation setup but invents the claim that forbidden-apis 'resolves sources from the Java source set' — it is a bytecode checker on output.classesDirs, so :core is fully covered and the 'silently excluded' claim is an absence-fabrication. v6 is cleaner: it catches the forbiddenApisTest gap, the ignoreFailures concern, and the accidental EOF-newline removal, but loses a point for fabricated suppression mechanics (signaturesFiles is not a suppression file; @SuppressWarnings is source-retention and invisible to a bytecode analyzer). Both missed that the unversioned 'jdk-unsafe' signature floats with the build JDK because Kafka sets options.release rather than targetCompatibility. v6 clearly outperforms v5 here.
